title: Multicycle RV32M arithmetic unit
motivation:
The current ALU computes every multiply and divide result combinationally in
the EX stage, including signed and unsigned 64-bit products plus divider and
remainder operators. That wide arithmetic cone is a likely Fmax limiter on
Tang Nano 20K, while CoreMark's hot path should not need single-cycle DIV/REM
throughput. Trading rare M-extension latency for a shorter common EX critical
path should improve iterations/second through higher clock frequency.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current ALU computes every multiply and divide result combinationally in
the EX stage, including signed and unsigned 64-bit products plus divider and
remainder operators. That wide arithmetic cone is a likely Fmax limiter on
Tang Nano 20K, while CoreMark's hot path should not need single-cycle DIV/REM
throughput. Trading rare M-extension latency for a shorter common EX critical
path should improve iterations/second through higher clock frequency.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split RV32M operations out of the always-combinational ALU path behind a
small multicycle M-unit handshake in EX. Keep ADD/SUB/logic/branch/address
operations purely combinational, but when the decoded alu_op is MUL/DIV/REM,
hold ID/EX and EX/MEM until the iterative arithmetic result is ready, then
retire normally through the existing MEM/WB and RVFI path. This should remove
divider and large multiplier logic from the ordinary EX-stage timing path;
the added stalls only affect uncommon M instructions and should be outweighed
by improved Fmax on the FPGA fitness metric.

title: Resolve control flow in ID
motivation: CoreMark spends a significant fraction of its bracketed runtime in tight loops and conditional control flow. The current five-stage pipeline resolves branches and jumps in EX, then flushes both IF and ID, so every taken branch or jump pays roughly a two-cycle redirect penalty. Reducing that penalty improves iterations/cycle without increasing retirement width or touching the external NRET=1 contract.

This should cut the common taken-control penalty from two bubbles to one while preserving RVFI order, trap reporting, and single-channel retirement.

title: Static BTFNT branch predictor
motivation: The current five-stage core always fetches sequentially and resolves every branch or jump in EX, so each taken loop branch pays a two-instruction front-end flush. CoreMark has tight loops and many backward branches, making a small static predictor likely to improve iterations per cycle without adding much FPGA area or hurting the ALU critical path.

title: Add fetch skid buffer
motivation: The current front end has no real IF/ID storage: the IF payload is combinational from the current PC and imem data, and an imem-not-ready cycle injects a bubble directly into decode. With the tournament cosim using roughly 22% random instruction stalls, CoreMark loses many cycles even when the back end is already stalled by load-use, dmem, or the multicycle M unit. A small fetch buffer is a structural way to recover iterations/cycle without revisiting the prior broken ID-stage branch-resolution path or adding fetch-path target adders that hurt Fmax.

title: Source-aware load-use stalls
motivation:
The current load-use hazard detector compares a load destination against the
raw rs1 and rs2 bitfields of the following instruction, regardless of whether
that instruction actually consumes those operands. For I-type immediates,
loads, JALR, LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK, some of those fields are
immediates or destination bits rather than true sources. Any accidental match
with the preceding load rd inserts an unnecessary bubble, directly reducing
CoreMark iterations per cycle without improving correctness.

load-heavy code while adding only small opcode decode logic off the main ALU
and memory datapaths, so Fmax should be largely preserved.

title: Decode-stage JAL predictor
motivation:
The prior predictor attempts regressed because they placed branch target decode,
target adders, return detection, and prediction muxes directly on the IF PC path.
CoreMark still pays the existing EX redirect penalty for every unconditional JAL,
and JAL targets are PC-relative with no register dependency or branch condition.
Predicting only JAL in decode should recover one control-flow bubble for calls and
long jumps while avoiding the risky fetch-path predictor logic that hurt Fmax.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a narrow decode-stage always-taken predictor for legal JAL instructions only.
ID computes pc+imm for JAL, redirects fetch one cycle before EX would, and records
the predicted next PC in the ID/EX bundle. EX then suppresses the normal JAL redirect
when the resolved jump target matches the predicted target, but retains the existing
EX recovery path for conditional branches, JALR, illegal instructions, and any target
mismatch. Because the predictor excludes branches and returns, it needs no B-type
immediate decode in IF, no RAS state, and no operand forwarding, making it a smaller

title: Source-qualified load interlock
motivation:
The current load-use detector compares the preceding load rd against both raw
source bitfields of the following instruction, even when those bits are not
architecturally consumed. CoreMark has many loads and immediate/control
instructions, so accidental rd matches can inject bubbles that reduce
iterations per cycle without protecting any real dependency.

title: Drop regfile reset fanout
motivation:
The current register file resets all 32 architectural registers with a for-loop,
which forces reset muxing or reset routing onto the whole small RF array. After
the M-unit split, remaining FPGA Fmax is likely limited by ordinary datapath
and control fanout rather than rare arithmetic. CoreMark initializes the state
it uses, x0 is already hardwired on reads, and RVFI correctness depends on
retired architectural behavior rather than power-on values of unused registers.

the synchronous write path for nonzero destination registers. Reads of x0 stay
forced to zero, same-cycle write-first bypass stays unchanged, and reset of the
pipeline control/valid bits still prevents spurious retirement after reset.
This should reduce reset fanout and per-register muxing in a timing-sensitive,
always-active block without changing CPI, branch behavior, memory behavior, or
the NRET=1 RVFI interface. The change is deliberately narrower than prior
failed micro-opts: it does not touch hazard detection, RVFI operand reporting,
MEM load extraction, or any front-end prediction path.

title: Ordered one-entry store buffer
motivation:
Recent structural attempts spent timing and LUTs on the front end or on load
bypass paths, and those changes either regressed fitness or broke stall
handling. The current core still freezes the entire pipeline whenever a store
reaches MEM and dmem_ready is low, even though independent ALU, branch, and
register-only instructions behind that store could execute while the store is
waiting. CoreMark has frequent data stores and the apples-to-apples cosim
injects random dmem stalls, so store backpressure is a promising structural
target that avoids fetch-path target adders and load-result critical muxes.

existing blocking behavior so CoreMark timing brackets remain exact. This
should recover cycles lost to random store stalls with modest control and no
new IF critical-path logic.

counters, or RAS control on the fetch path. A predictor for this slot should

title: Prune dead EX/MEM branch metadata

boundary, so pruning them is a low-risk micro-optimization for LUT/FF count
and routing delay without changing CPI.

redirect path in one cycle. Retiming that boundary can target Fmax without adding a
predictor, memory ordering structure, or RVFI contract change.

RV32I pipeline.

motivation:

improve Fmax without changing the visible memory ordering contract.

title: Decode-only JAL redirect predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core regressed because they added target
adders, tag compares, return-stack state, or predicted-PC muxing directly to
the IF next-PC path. The current core still pays the EX redirect penalty for
every unconditional JAL, and JAL needs no operand forwarding or direction
prediction once its instruction reaches decode.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core regressed because they added target
adders, tag compares, return-stack state, or predicted-PC muxing directly to
the IF next-PC path. The current core still pays the EX redirect penalty for
every unconditional JAL, and JAL needs no operand forwarding or direction
prediction once its instruction reaches decode.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a decode-stage predictor for legal JAL instructions only. ID already has
the instruction, PC, and immediate generator output, so it can compute the JAL
target and redirect fetch one cycle earlier than EX without adding any logic
to the IF PC increment path. Carry a small predicted-next-PC flag/value into
ID/EX so EX suppresses its normal JAL redirect when the ID prediction matches,
while all conditional branches, JALR, illegal instructions, and mismatches use
the existing EX recovery path. This avoids the failed fetch-side predictor
structures while recovering one bubble on CoreMark calls and PC-relative jumps.

title: Share M-unit multiplier hardware
motivation:
The current best core already removed RV32M arithmetic from the ordinary EX
ALU path, but m_unit still builds three separate 64-bit multiplier products
in parallel for MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU signedness variants. CoreMark uses
M operations rarely enough that throughput beyond the existing one-cycle
registered M-unit is not the main limiter, while the extra DSP/LUT/routing
pressure can still depress FPGA Fmax or perturb placement of the hot RV32I
pipeline.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current best core already removed RV32M arithmetic from the ordinary EX
ALU path, but m_unit still builds three separate 64-bit multiplier products
in parallel for MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU signedness variants. CoreMark uses
M operations rarely enough that throughput beyond the existing one-cycle
registered M-unit is not the main limiter, while the extra DSP/LUT/routing
pressure can still depress FPGA Fmax or perturb placement of the hot RV32I
pipeline.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the parallel signed-signed, unsigned-unsigned, and signed-unsigned
product generation in m_unit with one shared signed multiplier fed by
sign/zero-extended operands selected from the M opcode. MUL can take the low
half of the same product because low 32 bits are independent of operand
signedness; MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU select the high half with the appropriate
operand extension. Keep the existing start/valid/clear handshake, one-cycle
M latency, DIV/REM behavior, and RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions unchanged.
This should trim multiplier area and routing without adding CPI, front-end
prediction logic, memory ordering state, or any RVFI-visible behavior change.

title: Register WB result at MEM boundary
motivation:
The recent structural attempts mostly lost fitness by adding control-flow
prediction, buffering, memory overlap, or deeper execute-stage interlocks.
A safer structural target is the existing MEM/WB-to-WB combinational path:
the selected writeback value is still muxed after the MEM/WB register, then
fans out to the regfile write port, same-cycle regfile bypass, EX forwarding,
and RVFI rd_wdata. Registering that selected value at the retirement boundary
should shorten a hot fanout path without changing CPI, branch timing, memory
ordering, or the NRET=1 external contract.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a preselected rd_wdata/writeback-result field to the MEM/WB pipeline
bundle and compute it inside mem_stage before the MEM/WB register captures.
For loads this field captures the sign/zero-extended load result; for ALU,
JAL, JALR, AUIPC, LUI, and M-extension operations it captures the existing
EX/MEM alu_result. Then simplify wb_stage into a pass-through of the
registered writeback value instead of a post-register mem_to_reg mux. This
structural retiming preserves the same retirement boundary and RVFI data, but
removes the load-vs-ALU mux from the next cycle's forwarding/regfile/RVFI
fanout cone, which may improve Tang Nano 20K Fmax with essentially no
iterations-per-cycle penalty.

title: Decode-stage backward branch predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts lost fitness when they added BTB, RAS, tag compare,
counter, or target-adder logic to the IF next-PC path. The current core still
pays a two-bubble EX redirect penalty on taken loop-closing branches, which
are the predictor opportunity most likely to matter for CoreMark. A predictor
that waits until decode can use the already-fetched instruction and decoded
branch control while keeping fetch as the current simple pc+4 path.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a static decode-stage predictor for legal backward conditional branches
only. ID uses the existing immediate generator and decoded is_branch signal to
recognize negative B-type offsets, computes pc+imm, and redirects fetch one
cycle earlier than EX would. Carry predicted-valid and predicted-next-PC into
ID/EX so EX suppresses the normal redirect when the branch is actually taken
to the predicted target, or redirects to pc+4 on the loop-exit not-taken
misprediction. Forward branches, JAL, JALR, illegal encodings, and all memory
or RVFI behavior stay on the existing path. This targets loop bubbles without
reintroducing the failed IF-side predictor structures.

title: Narrow MEM/WB control state
motivation:
Recent micro-optimization wins came from removing always-present FPGA timing
and routing pressure, while attempts that added prediction, bypassing, or
stall-hiding control usually regressed. The current MEM/WB register still
carries the full decode control bundle even though the writeback and RVFI
retirement logic only consume a few bits after the MEM stage. Pruning that
cold control state should reduce flop count, muxing, and routing without
changing CoreMark CPI or touching the external NRET=1 contract.

control subset containing only reg_write, mem_to_reg, and is_illegal. Keep
the existing full ctrl_t through ID/EX and EX/MEM, because the execute and
memory stages still need branch, memory width, sign-extension, and M-op
controls. In mem_stage, collapse ctrl_with_trap into the narrow MEM/WB
fields after misalignment handling, and update wb_stage and core RVFI trap
references to use the narrow fields. This should trim unused MEM/WB control
flops and fanout while preserving all memory masks/data, load results,
writeback behavior, RVFI order, and trap reporting.

title: Split RVFI shadow metadata from datapath
motivation: The best recent gains came from removing broad arithmetic and register fanout from hot paths, while most structural attempts regressed by adding prediction, buffering, memory overlap, or extra execute-stage interlocks. The current pipeline still carries a wide all-purpose struct through EX/MEM and MEM/WB, mixing hot execution data with RVFI-only metadata such as original source values, source register numbers, instruction bits, memory masks, and retired PC fields. Even when those fields are not part of the architectural datapath, they share the same pipeline registers, reset/hold enables, and module boundaries as timing-critical forwarding, memory, and writeback signals.

Even when those fields are not part of the architectural datapath, they share the same pipeline registers, reset/hold enables, and module boundaries as timing-critical forwarding, memory, and writeback signals.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Split the pipeline state into a narrow execution payload and a parallel RVFI shadow payload. Keep the hot EX/MEM and MEM/WB datapath bundles limited to control bits and values needed for forwarding, dmem, writeback, and stalls, while carrying RVFI-only fields in a separate shadow register chain that uses the same valid, flush, hold, trap, and redirect decisions. This should reduce register width, enable fanout, and routing pressure on the timing-critical datapath without changing CPI, branch resolution timing, memory ordering, RVFI contents, or the NRET=1 top-level contract.

title: Decode return target predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core mostly regressed because they added
target adders, tag compares, counters, return-stack state, or predicted-PC
muxes directly to the IF next-PC path. The current core still pays the EX
redirect penalty on every canonical function return, and CoreMark contains
enough call/return traffic that recovering one redirect cycle there may help
iterations per cycle. A return predictor that operates only after decode can
use the already-fetched instruction, regfile read data, and existing immediate
path while keeping fetch's common pc+4 path untouched.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core mostly regressed because they added
target adders, tag compares, counters, return-stack state, or predicted-PC
muxes directly to the IF next-PC path. The current core still pays the EX
redirect penalty on every canonical function return, and CoreMark contains
enough call/return traffic that recovering one redirect cycle there may help
iterations per cycle. A return predictor that operates only after decode can
use the already-fetched instruction, regfile read data, and existing immediate
path while keeping fetch's common pc+4 path untouched.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a decode-stage predictor for canonical returns only: legal JALR with
rd=x0, funct3=0, imm=0, and rs1=x1 or x5. ID computes the return target from
the already-read link register value and redirects fetch one cycle before EX
would, but only when the base register is not the destination of an unresolved
older instruction in ID/EX or EX/MEM. Carry a minimal predicted-valid and
predicted-next-PC field into ID/EX so EX suppresses the normal JALR redirect
on an exact match and uses the existing recovery redirect on mismatches. This
avoids the failed IF-side RAS/BTB/BTFNT machinery while targeting a narrower
control-flow pattern with no branch direction ambiguity.

Detect canonical JALR returns, compute the register-based target from rs1_data plus zero immediate, and emit early redirect metadata only when the base operand is safe.
Wire ID-stage return prediction redirect into the existing IF/hazard redirect path and provide in-flight destination qualifiers for safe prediction gating.
Compare resolved JALR next PC against the decode prediction, suppress correct duplicate redirects, and retain existing EX recovery for mismatches and unpredicted returns.

lesson: - 2026-05-13 hyp-20260513-003-r9s2 (regression, -31.51%): Decode-stage return prediction still regressed because link-register hazard compares, early redirect arbitration, and predicted-PC fields added more hot control/datapath cost than CoreMark recovered from avoiding canonical JALR return bubbles.

title: Prune unused LUI control bit
motivation:
Recent wins came from removing always-present arithmetic, reset, and RVFI
routing pressure, while broader CPI-oriented changes and control prediction
usually regressed. The current control bundle still carries an is_lui bit
through decode and every pipeline stage even though LUI execution is already
fully encoded by alu_op=ALU_LUI and alu_src=1. That unused bit adds one more
decoded signal, pipeline flop, reset/hold mux input, and routing load across
hot stage boundaries without contributing to architectural behavior.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Remove the redundant is_lui signal from the decoder interface and ctrl_t,
and delete the corresponding ID-stage temporary and bundle assignment. Keep
LUI behavior unchanged by continuing to set alu_op=ALU_LUI, alu_src=1, and
reg_write=1 in the existing validated LUI decode arm. This is a narrow
no-CPI micro-optimization that should slightly reduce control-bundle width,
register enables, reset/hold muxing, and decode-to-EX routing pressure while
preserving the NRET=1 RVFI contract, trap discipline, memory behavior, and
all existing stall/forwarding logic.

title: Split MEM RVFI sideband path
motivation: The latest successful structural change split RVFI-only metadata away from the hot execution payloads and improved fitness without changing CPI. The remaining MEM stage still computes and registers RVFI memory sideband fields in the same combinational block and pipeline boundary that drives dmem, load extraction, writeback, and forwarding. A narrower structural separation in MEM is a safer follow-on than adding predictors, store buffers, load bypasses, or deeper execute retiming, all of which have regressed or broken previously.

hypothesis.motivation: The latest successful structural change split RVFI-only metadata away from the hot execution payloads and improved fitness without changing CPI. The remaining MEM stage still computes and registers RVFI memory sideband fields in the same combinational block and pipeline boundary that drives dmem, load extraction, writeback, and forwarding. A narrower structural separation in MEM is a safer follow-on than adding predictors, store buffers, load bypasses, or deeper execute retiming, all of which have regressed or broken previously.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Split the MEM-stage memory sideband work into a parallel RVFI-only path while keeping the execution path limited to dmem request signals, load_data, rd, compact control, and valid. The hot MEM/WB execution register should no longer share bundle assignment, hold, and mux fanout with RVFI-only mem_addr, mem_rmask, mem_wmask, mem_rdata, mem_wdata, pc, source values, and instruction fields. Preserve the existing single-retire MEM/WB retirement point, dmem stall behavior, misalignment traps, RVFI aligned-memory reporting, and NRET=1 port shape. This should reduce routing and enable fanout around the MEM boundary with little CPI risk, extending the proven RVFI-shadow split into the last stage where sideband metadata still sits next to timing-sensitive load/writeback logic.

title: Single hot loop branch predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core regressed because they added immediate
decode, target adders, return-stack state, counters, or multi-entry tag logic
to the fetch/decode control paths. CoreMark still spends cycles on repeated
taken loop-closing branches, but the predictor must be smaller than the prior
BTB, RAS, JAL, and decode-backward experiments to have any chance of paying
on Tang Nano 20K fitness.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core regressed because they added immediate
decode, target adders, return-stack state, counters, or multi-entry tag logic
to the fetch/decode control paths. CoreMark still spends cycles on repeated
taken loop-closing branches, but the predictor must be smaller than the prior
BTB, RAS, JAL, and decode-backward experiments to have any chance of paying
on Tang Nano 20K fitness.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add the smallest possible EX-trained predictor for one hot taken conditional
branch: store a single branch PC and its already-resolved target when EX sees
a taken backward branch, predict that target only on an exact PC match, and
invalidate the entry when the same branch later resolves not-taken. Unlike the
failed BTFNT and BTB attempts, IF performs no instruction immediate decode and
no target addition; it only compares the current PC with one registered PC and
muxes between pc+4 and one registered target. Carry a one-bit predicted flag
and predicted next PC to EX so correct predictions suppress the normal taken
redirect while mismatches use the existing recovery path. This targets the most
common loop bubble with minimal predictor state and avoids JAL/JALR/RAS logic.

title: Narrow EX/MEM control payload
motivation: The current best core already benefits from splitting RVFI shadow metadata away from hot execution payloads, but the EX/MEM execution register still carries the full decoded ctrl_t bundle after execute has consumed most of it. Branch, jump, operand-select, ALU-op, and AUIPC/LUI control bits are no longer needed once the EX result, write data, redirect, and RVFI pc_next have been produced. Trimming those cold bits at the EX/MEM boundary should reduce flop count, hold/reset muxing, and routing pressure on the memory, forwarding, and stall paths without changing CPI.

title: Handshake IFID register
motivation:
The current front end feeds imem_data directly into decode, immediate generation,
regfile read-address selection, load-use hazard checks, and the ID/EX register in
one cycle. Prior structural attempts that added prediction, bypassing, store
buffering, or EX retiming regressed by adding hot mux/control paths. The earlier
IF/ID-register attempt broke because it lacked a real consume/valid handshake,
not because the imem-to-decode retiming target was inherently invalid.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a proper registered IF/ID boundary with an explicit valid bit and ID consume
handshake. IF captures a fetched instruction only when imem is ready and the IF/ID
slot is empty or ID is consuming it; ID consumes exactly one valid entry when the

combinational path and improve Fmax, with only a small CPI risk and no change to

title: Fall-through redirect elision
motivation:
Every prior predictor that added fetch-side target arithmetic, tags, counters,
return-stack state, or predicted-PC metadata has regressed or broken this core.
The current front end is already a static not-taken predictor for conditional
branches, but EX still treats any resolved taken branch or jump as a redirect
and flushes younger stages even when the resolved target equals the fall-through
PC that fetch already predicted. Avoiding those false redirects is a predictor
refinement with almost no new state or fetch-path cost.

In EX, compute the sequential fall-through PC as pc+4 and suppress redirect
when the resolved branch or jump target equals that fall-through address. The
instruction should still retire with rvfi_pc_wdata equal to the architecturally
resolved target, but the front end does not need to flush because it has already
fetched the correct next instruction under the existing not-taken prediction.
This keeps all prediction logic out of IF and avoids carrying predicted-next-PC
metadata, while recovering bubbles from short JALs, degenerate branches, and
any compiler-generated control transfers whose target is the next instruction.

motivation:

without adding predictors, memory ordering structures, bypass selects, or an
extra execute stage.

title: Guarded hot-loop predictor retry
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core have consistently regressed or broken
because target adders, tags, counters, RAS state, or predicted-PC metadata
lengthened hot fetch/decode/control paths. The only predictor already present
and essentially free is static sequential fetch, but CoreMark still pays two
bubbles on every taken loop-closing branch. The one predictor angle still
worth testing is a corrected, extremely small hot-loop predictor that avoids
target arithmetic in fetch and explicitly fixes the prior pc_fwd failure.

title: Localize WB-to-ID bypass
motivation:
Prior structural attempts show that adding predictors, skid buffers, store buffers,
load bypasses, or deeper execute retiming usually costs more FPGA fitness than it
recovers. The remaining promising structural direction is to reduce always-active
fanout and muxing without changing CPI or control-flow timing. The integer regfile
currently embeds write-first same-cycle bypass compares and 32-bit muxes on both
combinational read ports, so WB write-enable/address/data fan out through the
register file as well as to forwarding, RVFI, and writeback.

title: Retimed M-unit operand stage
motivation: The largest proven bench-core gains came from keeping rare RV32M arithmetic off the ordinary RV32I execute path, and the smaller multiplier-sharing win suggests M-unit placement and timing still matter on the Tang Nano 20K. The current M unit still evaluates multiply/divide/remainder results directly from EX operands into the result register on the start edge, so a wide arithmetic cone remains a possible global Fmax limiter even though CoreMark rarely needs M-op throughput.

title: Source-aware load interlock
motivation:
The current five-stage pipeline still treats both raw rs bitfields of the
instruction in IF/ID as real consumers when deciding whether a preceding load
must insert a bubble. That is structurally conservative but overly broad:
OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR, LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK either ignore rs2 or
ignore both source fields, so accidental matches against the load rd can
create false load-use stalls. CoreMark is load-heavy, and reducing bubbles
without adding predictors, MEM bypass muxes, store buffers, or deeper EX
staging is one of the few remaining CPI improvements with modest timing risk.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny source-use classifier for the IF/ID instruction and feed its
uses_rs1 and uses_rs2 bits only into the load-use hazard comparison. Keep the
existing one-cycle interlock for real consumers, but suppress the stall when
the matching raw bitfield is actually an immediate, destination, or unused
SYSTEM/FENCE field. This should improve iterations/cycle by removing false
bubbles while leaving RVFI rs1/rs2 address and data reporting unchanged, which
avoids the failure mode from earlier source-aware attempts. The added opcode
decode is local to hazard control and should be cheaper than prior structural
approaches that put adders, predictors, or new bypass selects on hot paths.

title: Static not-taken fast path
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core have consistently regressed or broken
because they added fetch-side target adders, tag compares, predicted-PC
metadata, return-stack state, or decode-stage redirect arbitration. The only
predictor that has not hurt timing is the one already implicit in the core:
sequential static not-taken fetch for conditional branches. The remaining
predictor opportunity is therefore to make that static predictor cheaper in
EX rather than add new prediction state.

sequential static not-taken fetch for conditional branches. The remaining
predictor opportunity is therefore to make that static predictor cheaper in
EX rather than add new prediction state.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure EX branch resolution around the static not-taken fast path. For
conditional branches, compute the resolved next PC as pc+4 by default and
only feed the branch-target adder result into redirect and RVFI pc_next when
the branch condition is true. Keep jumps, JALR, misalignment traps, RVFI
reporting, and the existing flush/stall policy unchanged. This does not save
taken-branch bubbles, but it may shorten the common not-taken branch/control
cone and reduce target-adder fanout without any IF predictor mux, stored
target, predicted-PC payload, or new recovery path.

title: Stall-safe prebranch loop predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts usually lost fitness by adding same-cycle fetch target
adders, tag arrays, counters, RAS state, or decode-stage redirect arbitration.
The best remaining predictor angle is still the hot CoreMark loop-closing
branch, but the implementation must keep the branch-fetch PC path simple and
explicitly avoid the stale-prediction and RVFI pc_next failure modes seen in
earlier single-entry loop predictor retries.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior predictor attempts usually lost fitness by adding same-cycle fetch target
adders, tag arrays, counters, RAS state, or decode-stage redirect arbitration.
The best remaining predictor angle is still the hot CoreMark loop-closing
branch, but the implementation must keep the branch-fetch PC path simple and
explicitly avoid the stale-prediction and RVFI pc_next failure modes seen in

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry EX-trained backward-branch predictor that stores branch_pc,
prebranch_pc = branch_pc - 4, and the resolved target. IF compares the current
PC against prebranch_pc and registers a one-cycle pending prediction; when the
following branch PC is actually fetched, the next-PC mux uses only that pending
bit and the stored target, not a same-cycle tag compare, immediate decode, or
target adder. The pending bit must advance only when IF advances, be cleared on
stalls/flushes/redirects, and be carried to EX so correct predicted-taken loop
branches suppress the normal redirect while loop exits invalidate the entry and
redirect to pc+4 with RVFI pc_next matching the next retired instruction.
